Apple Apple Scab.description: Caused by Venturia inaequalis. Produces dark, scabby lesions on leaves and fruit. Thrives in cool, wet spring weather.
treatment: Apply Captan 50% WP at 2g per litre of water
Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre during early infection
Use Myclobutanil-based fungicide for active infections
Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves and fruit
Prune trees to improve air circulation
prevention: Plant scab-resistant apple varieties (e.g., Liberty, Enterprise)
Apply dormant-season copper spray before bud break
Maintain proper tree spacing for airflow
Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn

Apple Black Rot.description: Caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa. Creates circular brown lesions on fruit and frogeye leaf spots. Overwinters in mummified fruit.
treatment: Apply Captan or Thiophanate-methyl fungicide
Remove all mummified fruit from trees and ground
Prune out dead or cankered wood during dormancy
Apply copper-based spray at green tip stage
Spray Myclobutanil at petal fall and 2 weeks after
prevention: Remove mummified fruit before spring
Maintain good tree hygiene and sanitation
Prune for open canopy and air circulation
Monitor and remove fire blight cankers

Apple Cedar Apple Rust.description: Caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. Requires both apple and cedar/juniper hosts. Creates orange spots on leaves.
treatment: Apply Myclobutanil at pink bud stage through petal fall
Spray Mancozeb at 2g/litre as preventive measure
Apply Triadimefon-based fungicide when symptoms appear
Remove any nearby cedar or juniper trees within 1km if possible
Apply sulfur-based fungicide during early growth
prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties
Remove cedar/juniper galls before they sporulate in spring
Maintain distance from juniper windbreaks
Apply preventive fungicide sprays from pink bud onwards

Apple Healthy.description: No disease detected. The apple leaf appears healthy with normal coloration and structure.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue regular maintenance and monitoring
Maintain balanced fertilization schedule
prevention: Continue regular monitoring for early disease signs
Maintain proper irrigation and nutrition
Practice good orchard sanitation
Apply preventive dormant-season sprays

Blueberry Healthy.description: No disease detected. The blueberry leaf appears healthy with normal growth patterns.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Maintain acidic soil pH between 4.5-5.5
Continue regular watering and mulching
prevention: Maintain soil acidity with sulfur amendments
Use pine bark mulch to retain moisture
Prune annually to remove old canes
Monitor for pest infestations regularly

Cherry Powdery Mildew.description: Caused by Podosphaera clandestina. Creates white powdery coating on leaves, shoots, and fruit. Reduces photosynthesis.
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicide at 3g/litre of water
Spray Trifloxystrobin at first sign of infection
Use potassium bicarbonate solution as organic alternative
Apply neem oil at 5ml/litre for mild infections
Remove heavily infected shoots and destroy
prevention: Plant resistant cherry varieties
Ensure proper spacing for air movement
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Water at base of tree, avoid wetting foliage

Cherry Healthy.description: No disease detected. The cherry leaf appears healthy with vibrant green coloration.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue standard cherry tree care practices
Maintain proper pruning schedule
prevention: Regular monitoring for disease symptoms
Maintain balanced nutrient program
Ensure adequate drainage around root zone
Apply dormant oil spray in late winter

Corn Cercospora Leaf Spot.description: Caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. Creates rectangular gray-tan lesions between leaf veins. Reduces photosynthetic area significantly.
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin (Quadris) at 6ml/litre of water
Spray Pyraclostrobin + Metconazole combination fungicide
Apply foliar fungicide at VT/R1 growth stage
Use Propiconazole at 1ml/litre for active infection
Rotate with non-corn crops for at least 1 year
prevention: Plant resistant corn hybrids
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops
Manage crop residue through tillage
Maintain balanced nitrogen and potassium fertility

Corn Common Rust.description: Caused by Puccinia sorghi. Creates small circular to elongate cinnamon-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre of water
Spray Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/litre
Apply Azoxystrobin-based fungicide at early infection
Use Triazole fungicides for severe infections
Remove lower infected leaves to reduce inoculum
prevention: Plant rust-resistant corn hybrids
Early planting to avoid peak rust season
Monitor fields from V8 stage onwards
Balanced fertilization to maintain plant vigor

Corn Northern Leaf Blight.description: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum. Creates large cigar-shaped gray-green lesions 2.5-15cm long on leaves.
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole at tassel emergence
Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre preventively
Use Pyraclostrobin-based fungicide for active infection
Apply foliar fungicide before or at disease onset
Remove and destroy crop residue after harvest
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids with Ht gene resistance
Rotate crops - avoid corn-on-corn
Reduce surface residue through tillage
Maintain proper plant population and spacing

Corn Healthy.description: No disease detected. The corn leaf appears healthy with uniform green coloration.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue standard corn management practices
Monitor for pest and disease pressure
prevention: Maintain proper fertility program
Scout fields regularly for disease symptoms
Practice crop rotation
Manage irrigation to avoid water stress

Grape Black Rot.description: Caused by Guignardia bidwellii. Creates brown circular lesions on leaves and mummifies berries. Devastating to untreated vineyards.
treatment: Apply Myclobutanil at 0.5ml/litre starting at shoot growth
Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2g/litre pre-bloom
Use Captan 50% WP at 2g/litre post-bloom
Remove and destroy mummified berries and infected shoots
Apply fungicide at 7-10 day intervals during wet weather
prevention: Remove mummified fruit from vines and ground
Prune for open canopy and air circulation
Apply dormant Lime Sulfur spray
Begin fungicide program at 6-inch shoot growth

Grape Esca Black Measles.description: Caused by complex of fungi including Phaeomoniella chlamydospora. Causes interveinal striping and eventual vine death.
treatment: Apply Thiophanate-methyl to pruning wounds within 24 hours
Trunk injection with fungicide for established infections
Remedial trunk surgery to remove infected wood
Apply sodium arsenite (where legally permitted)
Retrain new cordons from healthy suckers
prevention: Protect pruning wounds with wound sealant
Prune during dry weather to prevent spore entry
Use clean, disinfected pruning tools
Double pruning technique to delay wound exposure

Grape Leaf Blight.description: Caused by Pseudocercospora vitis (Isariopsis). Creates dark brown angular lesions with yellow halos on older leaves.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre of water
Spray Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 3g/litre
Use Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/litre for severe infections
Apply Chlorothalonil at 2g/litre as alternative
Remove severely infected leaves to reduce spread
prevention: Maintain good vineyard ventilation through canopy management
Avoid overhead irrigation
Balance nitrogen application to avoid lush growth
Apply preventive fungicide before monsoon season

Grape Healthy.description: No disease detected. The grape leaf appears healthy with normal shape and coloration.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue regular vineyard maintenance
Maintain balanced nutrition program
prevention: Continue canopy management for airflow
Monitor regularly for early disease symptoms
Follow preventive spray schedule
Maintain proper drainage

Orange Haunglongbing Citrus Greening.description: Caused by Candidatus Liberibacter bacteria, spread by Asian citrus psyllid. Produces blotchy mottled leaves and lopsided bitter fruit.
treatment: No cure exists - manage to extend tree productivity
Apply systemic insecticides (Imidacloprid) for psyllid control
Use foliar nutritional sprays to maintain tree health
Apply Enhanced Foliar Nutrition (EFN) program
Remove and destroy severely infected trees to prevent spread
prevention: Control Asian citrus psyllid populations aggressively
Use certified disease-free nursery stock
Apply reflective mulch to repel psyllids
Implement area-wide psyllid management programs

Peach Bacterial Spot.description: Caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. Creates angular water-soaked lesions that become necrotic. Defoliates trees.
treatment: Apply Copper hydroxide 77% WP at 2g/litre at petal fall
Spray Oxytetracycline at 1g/litre during bloom
Use Copper + Mancozeb combination at 2g/litre
Apply bactericide sprays at 7-10 day intervals
Reduce nitrogen to slow canopy growth
prevention: Plant resistant peach varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation and wetting foliage
Maintain proper tree spacing and pruning
Apply copper sprays at leaf fall and before bud break

Peach Healthy.description: No disease detected. The peach leaf appears healthy with normal coloration.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue regular peach tree care
Maintain balanced fertilization
prevention: Regular monitoring for disease and pest symptoms
Apply dormant-season copper spray
Prune annually for air circulation
Maintain proper irrigation schedule

Pepper Bell Bacterial Spot.description: Caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria. Creates small dark water-soaked lesions on leaves and fruit.
treatment: Apply Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 3g/litre
Spray Streptomycin sulfate at 0.5g/litre (where permitted)
Use Copper hydroxide + Mancozeb combination
Apply Acibenzolar-S-methyl for systemic resistance
Remove and destroy severely infected plants
prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds and transplants
Practice crop rotation (3-year minimum)
Avoid working in wet fields
Disinfect tools and equipment between fields

Pepper Bell Healthy.description: No disease detected. The bell pepper leaf appears healthy with normal green coloration.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue standard pepper care practices
Maintain adequate calcium supply to prevent blossom end rot
prevention: Monitor for pests and diseases regularly
Maintain consistent watering schedule
Apply balanced fertilizer
Provide adequate support for heavy fruit

Potato Early Blight.description: Caused by Alternaria solani. Creates concentric ring target-shaped brown lesions on older leaves. Progresses upward through canopy.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre of water
Spray Chlorothalonil at 2g/litre at first symptom
Use Azoxystrobin at 1ml/litre for active infection
Apply Difenoconazole at 0.5ml/litre for severe cases
Remove lower infected leaves to slow spread
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Practice 3-year crop rotation
Maintain balanced nitrogen fertility
Irrigate in morning to allow leaf drying

Potato Late Blight.description: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Creates water-soaked dark lesions with white sporulation on leaf undersides. Extremely destructive.
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) at 2.5g/litre
Spray Cymoxanil + Mancozeb (Curzate M) at 3g/litre
Use Dimethomorph 50% WP at 1g/litre for active infection
Apply Mandipropamid at 0.6ml/litre as curative spray
Destroy all infected plant material - do not compost
prevention: Plant certified late blight resistant varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation
Hill soil around potato stems to protect tubers
Begin preventive fungicide spray when humidity exceeds 80%

Potato Healthy.description: No disease detected. The potato leaf appears healthy with normal growth.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue balanced fertilization program
Maintain proper hilling schedule
prevention: Monitor for early and late blight symptoms regularly
Use certified seed potatoes
Practice crop rotation
Maintain proper soil drainage

Raspberry Healthy.description: No disease detected. The raspberry leaf appears healthy with normal coloration.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue regular raspberry cane management
Maintain mulch around base
prevention: Remove spent floricanes after harvest
Maintain proper spacing between canes
Apply dormant-season lime sulfur spray
Monitor for viral symptoms

Rice Brown Spot.description: Caused by Bipolaris oryzae. Creates oval brown spots with gray centers on leaves. Associated with nutrient-deficient soils.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre of water
Spray Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/litre
Use Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/litre
Apply Tricyclazole 75% WP at 0.6g/litre
Correct soil nutrient deficiencies, especially potassium
prevention: Use balanced fertilization especially potassium
Treat seeds with fungicide before sowing
Maintain proper water management in paddies
Use resistant rice varieties

Rice Hispa.description: Caused by Dicladispa armigera (rice hispa beetle). Adults scrape upper leaf surface; larvae mine between leaf tissues.
treatment: Apply Chlorpyrifos 20% EC at 2.5ml/litre
Spray Quinalphos 25% EC at 2ml/litre on affected areas
Use Cartap Hydrochloride 4G granules at 25kg/ha
Manually remove and destroy infected leaf tips
Apply neem-based formulation at 5ml/litre
prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen application
Remove grassy weeds from field margins
Clip leaf tips during transplanting if eggs are visible
Use resistant rice varieties

Rice Leaf Blast.description: Caused by Magnaporthe oryzae. Creates diamond-shaped lesions with gray centers on leaves. Can destroy entire crop if untreated.
treatment: Apply Tricyclazole 75% WP at 0.6g/litre of water immediately
Spray Isoprothiolane 40% EC at 1.5ml/litre
Use Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/litre as alternative
Apply Kasugamycin 3% SL at 2ml/litre for bacterial component
Drain field and reduce nitrogen application
prevention: Plant blast-resistant rice varieties
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation
Use seed treatment with Tricyclazole before sowing

Soybean Healthy.description: No disease detected. The soybean leaf appears healthy with normal trifoliate structure.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue standard soybean management practices
Monitor for pest pressure
prevention: Practice crop rotation with non-legume crops
Monitor for soybean rust and sudden death syndrome
Maintain proper soil pH for nitrogen fixation
Scout fields weekly during reproductive stages

Squash Powdery Mildew.description: Caused by Podosphaera xanthii. Creates white powdery patches on leaf surfaces. Reduces fruit quality and yield.
treatment: Apply sulfur-based fungicide at 3g/litre of water
Spray Myclobutanil at 0.5ml/litre for active infection
Use potassium bicarbonate at 5g/litre as organic option
Apply neem oil at 5ml/litre weekly
Spray milk solution (40% milk) as home remedy
prevention: Plant powdery mildew resistant varieties
Ensure proper plant spacing for airflow
Avoid overhead watering
Remove and destroy infected leaves early

Strawberry Leaf Scorch.description: Caused by Diplocarpon earlianum. Creates irregular purple blotches on leaves that merge and cause leaf drying (scorch).
treatment: Apply Captan 50% WP at 2g/litre of water
Spray Myclobutanil at 0.5ml/litre at first symptom
Use Copper hydroxide at 2g/litre after renovation
Remove and destroy severely infected leaves
Renovate beds by mowing after harvest
prevention: Plant resistant strawberry varieties
Ensure good air circulation between plants
Use drip irrigation, avoid wetting leaves
Renovate strawberry beds annually after harvest

Strawberry Healthy.description: No disease detected. The strawberry leaf appears healthy with vibrant green color.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue regular strawberry care
Maintain runners and bed management
prevention: Monitor for leaf spot and mildew symptoms
Maintain straw mulch for weed and splash control
Replace plants every 3-4 years
Ensure proper drainage

Tomato Bacterial Spot.description: Caused by Xanthomonas vesicatoria. Creates small dark water-soaked spots on leaves that become necrotic with yellow halos.
treatment: Apply Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 3g/litre of water
Spray Streptomycin sulfate at 0.5g/litre (where permitted)
Use Copper hydroxide + Mancozeb (2g + 2.5g per litre)
Apply Acibenzolar-S-methyl for host resistance induction
Remove severely infected plants from field
prevention: Use pathogen-free seed and transplants
Practice 2-3 year crop rotation
Avoid overhead irrigation
Disinfect stakes, ties, and equipment

Tomato Early Blight.description: Caused by Alternaria solani. Creates concentric ring (bull's-eye) lesions starting on lower older leaves. Progressive defoliation.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre of water
Spray Chlorothalonil at 2g/litre at 7-day intervals
Use Azoxystrobin at 1ml/litre for active infection
Apply Difenoconazole at 0.5ml/litre in severe cases
Remove lower infected leaves and destroy
prevention: Stake or cage plants for air circulation
Mulch soil to prevent splash-borne infection
Rotate crops on 3-year cycle
Water at base of plants in morning

Tomato Late Blight.description: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Rapidly spreading water-soaked dark lesions with white fuzzy sporulation underneath. Can destroy crop in days.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2g per litre of water
Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) at 2.5g/litre
Remove and destroy infected plant parts immediately
Avoid overhead irrigation; switch to drip irrigation
Apply copper-based fungicide as preventive spray
prevention: Use certified disease-resistant varieties
Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation
Rotate crops - avoid tomato/potato in same field
Monitor fields regularly during monsoon season

Tomato Leaf Mold.description: Caused by Passalora fulva. Creates pale green-yellow spots on upper leaf surface with olive-brown fuzzy mold beneath.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre of water
Spray Chlorothalonil at 2g/litre at 10-day intervals
Use Copper oxychloride at 3g/litre for greenhouse crops
Improve greenhouse ventilation immediately
Remove heavily infected lower leaves
prevention: Plant resistant tomato varieties (Cf gene resistance)
Ensure proper ventilation in greenhouses
Avoid high humidity - use drip irrigation
Space plants adequately for airflow

Tomato Septoria Leaf Spot.description: Caused by Septoria lycopersici. Creates numerous small circular spots with dark borders and gray centers with tiny black pycnidia.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre starting at first sign
Spray Chlorothalonil at 2g/litre at 7-10 day intervals
Use Copper oxychloride at 3g/litre as alternative
Apply Azoxystrobin at 1ml/litre for advanced infection
Remove infected lower leaves immediately
prevention: Mulch soil to prevent rain splash
Stake plants and prune lower branches
Rotate crops on 3-year cycle
Use drip irrigation to keep leaves dry

Tomato Spider Mites.description: Caused by Tetranychus urticae (two-spotted spider mite). Creates tiny yellow stippling on leaves, fine webbing visible. Thrives in hot dry conditions.
treatment: Spray Abamectin 1.9% EC at 0.5ml/litre of water
Apply Dicofol 18.5% EC at 2.5ml/litre
Use neem oil at 5ml/litre for mild infestations
Release Phytoseiulus persimilis predatory mites
Spray water forcefully on leaf undersides to dislodge mites
prevention: Maintain adequate plant hydration
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill predators
Monitor leaf undersides regularly with hand lens
Increase humidity around plants during hot weather

Tomato Target Spot.description: Caused by Corynespora cassiicola. Creates brown lesions with concentric rings and yellow halos, primarily on lower leaves.
treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil at 2g/litre of water
Spray Azoxystrobin at 1ml/litre at first symptoms
Use Difenoconazole at 0.5ml/litre for active infection
Apply Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/litre preventively
Remove and destroy infected lower foliage
prevention: Practice crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Manage plant density for air movement
Mulch soil to reduce splash dispersal
Prune lower branches and maintain plant hygiene

Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.description: Caused by TYLCV, transmitted by Bemisia tabaci (whitefly). Leaves curl upward, yellow margins, stunted growth. No cure exists.
treatment: No cure for viral infection - manage whitefly vector
Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL at 0.5ml/litre for whitefly control
Spray Thiamethoxam 25% WG at 0.3g/litre
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce whitefly
Remove and destroy infected plants to prevent spread
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties
Use reflective silver mulch to repel whiteflies
Install fine-mesh insect netting on nurseries
Maintain a crop-free period to break whitefly cycle

Tomato Mosaic Virus.description: Caused by ToMV. Creates mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves, leaf distortion, and reduced fruit set. Extremely stable virus.
treatment: No chemical cure exists for viral infections
Remove and destroy all infected plants immediately
Wash hands with soap before handling healthy plants
Disinfect tools with 10% bleach or TSP solution
Control aphid vectors with insecticidal soap
prevention: Use ToMV-resistant tomato varieties (Tm-2 gene)
Disinfect seeds with 10% TSP solution before sowing
Wash hands after handling tobacco products
Do not smoke near tomato plants
Sanitize greenhouse structures between crops

Tomato Healthy.description: No disease detected. The tomato leaf appears healthy with normal color and structure.
treatment: No treatment required - plant is healthy
Continue regular watering and fertilization
Maintain support structures as plant grows
prevention: Monitor plants weekly for disease symptoms
Maintain consistent watering to prevent stress
Apply balanced fertilizer per growth stage
Prune suckers for better air circulation
